sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 1.47 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.1)

statement.latex: A subgroup$H$of a group$G$is said to be strictly isolated\/ if, whenever$xg_1^{-1}xg_1 g_n^{-1}xg_n$belongs to$H$, so do$x$and each$g_i^{-1}xg_i$. A group in which the identity subgroup is strictly isolated is called an$S$-group. Do there exist$S$-groups that are not orderable groups? { A.\,I.\,Kokorin} Yes, such groups do exist (V.\,V.\,Bludov, Algebra and Logic, { 13} (1974), 343--360).

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory IV_PROBLEM_UNDEFINED; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
